agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics system with an established production domain model governing Shipment-to-Carrier assignment. The product team is requesting an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments (>500 kg) across multiple carriers without supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to fulfill a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a derivative Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The act of binding exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once locked, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role authorized to reassign Carriers on locked Shipments."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed behavior where the system autonomously divides a Shipment exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each with a different Carrier, bypassing Supervisor approval."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is handed off for transport. The system guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. If a Shipment exists without exactly one Carrier, or with multiple Carriers, the system is in a corrupt state — accountability, tracking, billing, and liability are all undefined.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in the system, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, the assignment is locked and only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and authorization constraint. Unsupervised reassignment could lead to lost shipments, double-handling, or accountability gaps. The Supervisor gate exists to enforce operational control.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to reassign a Carrier on a locked Shipment without Supervisor authorization must be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical constraint — a shipment leaves on one truck with one responsible carrier. Violating this at dispatch means the goods have no single accountable party.","testable_assertion":"No Shipment can transition to 'dispatched' status unless it has exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment weighing exactly 500 kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the contradiction is resolved. Boundary condition needs explicit specification."},{"scenario":"A Shipment already assigned to a Carrier is later updated to exceed 500 kg (e.g., items added).","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. Would auto-split require removing the locked Carrier assignment? That violates BR-2."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting.","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting? Undefined. The concept of sub-shipment does not exist in the domain model."},{"scenario":"A Carrier is assigned, then the Shipment is split — what happens to the original Carrier assignment?","expected_behavior":"The original assignment must either persist (violating the multi-carrier intent) or be revoked (violating BR-2 without Supervisor involvement)."},{"scenario":"Billing and liability: which Carrier is accountable for the original Shipment after a split?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The 1:1 Shipment-Carrier relationship underpins billing, liability, and tracking. Splitting breaks all three."},{"scenario":"Customer-facing tracking: customer placed one order, now sees multiple sub-shipments.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. UX, communication, and SLA implications are unaddressed."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Significant. The 1:1 Carrier-Shipment model underpins billing, insurance, and liability assignment. Splitting a Shipment across carriers without resolving who owns what creates invoicing ambiguity, potential double-billing, and unclear insurance liability on damaged goods.","operational":"High disruption. The Supervisor reassignment gate exists for operational control — bypassing it for auto-split introduces an uncontrolled mutation path. Dispatch workflows, warehouse handoff processes, and carrier coordination all assume one carrier per shipment.","user":"Customer confusion. A customer who booked one shipment now receives multiple tracking numbers. SLA enforcement becomes ambiguous — if one sub-shipment is late, is the whole shipment late?","risk":"Critical domain integrity risk. The proposed feature directly violates two production invariants. Shipping it as described would corrupt the foundational guarantee of the system: one shipment, one carrier, one accountable party.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Warehouse","impact":"Must handle multiple handoffs for what was one shipment. Picking, packing, and staging workflows need redesign."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive partial shipments. Contracts, pricing, and SLAs may not support partial loads created by automated splitting."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over reassignment is bypassed. Accountability and audit trail gaps emerge."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoice reconciliation breaks. One customer order now maps to N carrier invoices with no defined aggregation logic."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"See multiple shipments where they expected one. Tracking, delivery coordination, and returns become more complex."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Liability for goods in transit becomes ambiguous when split across carriers without explicit contractual framework."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction between two production invariants (1:1 Carrier assignment, Supervisor-only reassignment) and the requested feature (multi-carrier auto-split without Supervisor). This is not an ambiguity — it is a hard conflict. The feature as described cannot be implemented without violating the existing domain model. Confidence is low because the spec is self-contradicting and cannot be accepted as-is.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between the feature request and BR-1/BR-2 is unambiguous","The existing invariants are well-defined and production-proven","The stakeholder impact analysis is grounded in standard logistics domain knowledge"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax or replace the existing invariants","Whether 'sub-shipment' is meant to be a new aggregate root or a child entity","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard rule or a soft policy","What the billing, liability, and tracking model should look like post-split"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and invariants are intentional and well-understood by the organization","The product team may not have been aware of the invariant conflicts when drafting this request"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates two production invariants: (1) BR-1 — a Shipment must have exactly one Carrier, and (2) BR-2 — only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier. The feature requests multiple carriers per shipment and bypasses Supervisor authorization. These cannot coexist.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Team","Operations","Supervisors","Finance","Customers","Legal"],"options":["A: Reject the feature as incompatible with the domain model.","B: Introduce a new aggregate — e.g., 'ShipmentGroup' or 'SplitOrder' — that contains multiple independent Shipments, each preserving the 1:1 Carrier invariant. The parent entity tracks the logical grouping. Auto-split creates new Shipments, it does not assign multiple carriers to one Shipment.","C: Formally relax BR-1 and BR-2 with full stakeholder sign-off, redesigning billing, liability, tracking, and Supervisor authority. This is a major domain model evolution, not a feature.","D: Allow auto-split but require Supervisor approval for the split action, preserving BR-2 while introducing sub-shipments as a new concept under BR-1 (each sub-shipment still gets exactly one Carrier)."],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce a 'ShipmentGroup' aggregate that preserves all existing invariants. Each sub-shipment is a first-class Shipment with exactly one Carrier. The split operation creates new Shipments rather than violating the 1:1 constraint. This gives the product team the behavior they want without corrupting the domain model. Option D is a reasonable alternative if the business values the Supervisor gate for split decisions."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple Carriers to what is logically one Shipment, breaking the 1:1 Carrier-Shipment invariant.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor authority by automatically reassigning/splitting Carrier responsibility without Supervisor involvement.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can reassign."},{"violation":"At dispatch, a split Shipment would not have exactly one Carrier — it would have multiple Carriers across sub-shipments, with no defined single accountable party for the original Shipment.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: At dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier."}]}
